tools.summary: TrumpExcel Random Team Generator is a polished simple random splitter embedded in a spreadsheet-focused site. Paste names, choose number of groups or people per group, generate, shuffle again, then copy to clipboard, download CSV, or print a clean table. It runs locally according to the page copy, and the output is genuinely spreadsheet-friendly, but there are no constraints, balancing, leaders, repeat controls, or saved rosters.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A good basic splitter with strong spreadsheet-oriented exports. Privacy is the major drag because the surrounding site has a heavy consent/ad-tech layer.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy: paste names, choose split method, generate. The surrounding blog page is busy, but the embedded tool itself is clear.
ratingComments.design: The tool UI is clean and modern enough, but it sits inside a busy WordPress/Excel blog page with newsletter/promo/navigation clutter.
ratingComments.features: Basic: number-of-groups, people-per-group, live name count, shuffle again, reset. No constraints, balancing, multi-session repeats, leaders, attributes, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong for a simple splitter: copy-to-clipboard in spreadsheet-friendly columns, CSV download, and print view. No Zoom-specific export or share links.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad for sensitive rosters despite local tool processing. The page shows a consent dialog for 141 partners, including precise geolocation and ad/personalization purposes, plus Google tags and a heavy WordPress/ad-tech environment.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Standard random grouping. Fisher-Yates shuffle and spreadsheet output are useful, but result quality remains random-only.

pros: Runs locally according to page copy
tools.reviewNote: Playwright test generated 3 groups from 9 names after rejecting the consent dialog and verified Copy, Download CSV, Print, and Shuffle Again buttons.

tools.name: Zoom breakout room assignment
tools.bestFor: Assigning live meeting participants directly into Zoom rooms
tools.summary: Zoom breakout rooms are not a general group generator, but they are a real in-meeting grouping workflow. Hosts can create up to 100 rooms, auto-assign participants evenly, assign manually, let participants choose rooms, pre-assign rooms with a CSV, save assignments for recurring meetings, and move people during the session. It is convenient inside Zoom and weak everywhere else.
tools.pricing: Breakout rooms are available in Zoom meetings, including free/basic contexts, but meeting duration, participant capacity, admin controls, and large-meeting needs depend on Zoom plan and account settings.
tools.overallComment: Very useful if the only destination is a live Zoom meeting. Not a general-purpose group generator and not a quality optimizer.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy enough for hosts already comfortable with Zoom, but awkward as a group generator: breakout settings must be enabled, pre-assignment lives in the web portal, CSV setup uses email addresses, and participants often need to be signed in correctly.
ratingComments.design: The in-meeting breakout UI is functional and integrated into the meeting experience. It is not a dedicated planning interface, but it is much better than exporting from a randomizer and then manually recreating rooms.
ratingComments.features: Moderate for Zoom-specific grouping: automatic even assignment, manual assignment, participant self-select, room renaming, moving/swapping, up to 100 rooms, CSV pre-assignment, saved assignments for recurring meetings, timers, broadcast, and activity status. Missing general group-generator features: constraints, balancing attributes, repeat optimization, multi-session planning outside recurring-room saves, and diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: The output is directly actionable inside Zoom, which is valuable. Outside Zoom it is weak: pre-assignment CSV import exists, but there is no normal roster export, spreadsheet/report output, or shareable assignment page for other tools.
ratingComments.privacy: Zoom has serious enterprise/security documentation, DPAs, and education compliance materials, but this is still a hosted meeting platform processing participant identities, room names, telemetry/service data, chats/recordings depending on settings, and account/admin data. Better than ad-tech utilities, not private like a local tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: High friction for a “group generator”: the host needs a Zoom account/meeting, breakout settings enabled, and pre-assigned participants need matching Zoom accounts/emails for assignments to apply reliably.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Automatic assignment creates evenly sized rooms, which is fine for live one-off breakouts. It does not balance attributes, satisfy constraints, reduce repeats across varied sessions, or explain quality.

pros: Automatic, manual, and self-select room assignment
Can save breakout assignments for recurring meetings
Timers, broadcast, and activity status help live facilitation
cons: Pre-assignment depends on participant Zoom account emails
No repeat optimization across arbitrary sessions
tools.reviewNote: Review only Zoom breakout-room assignment, not Zoom as a full meeting product. It should score well for in-meeting execution but not for general group-generation quality.

tools.name: Google Sheets
tools.bestFor: Spreadsheet users willing to build their own random grouping template
tools.summary: Google Sheets can be made into a random group generator with formulas, random sorting, templates, or Apps Script. It is flexible and excellent for storing rosters and exporting results, but it is not a purpose-built group generator: users have to build or copy the grouping logic themselves.
tools.pricing: Free with a Google account for consumer use; Google Workspace and Workspace for Education plans vary by organization.
tools.overallComment: Powerful as a platform, mediocre as a group generator. It can outperform many tiny randomizers if you know Sheets, but the actual group-generation workflow is DIY.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not easy as a group generator. Entering a roster is easy, but random grouping requires formulas, sorting, a copied template, or Apps Script. Spreadsheet-comfortable users will be fine; everyone else gets setup work.
ratingComments.design: The spreadsheet interface is mature, readable, collaborative, and reliable. It is not tailored to group generation, so the UX depends entirely on the sheet/template you build.
ratingComments.features: Flexible but DIY. Sheets can handle arbitrary team counts, group sizes, saved rosters, notes, formulas, filters, imports, exports, and custom scripts. Attribute balancing, keep-apart rules, fixed placement, or repeats are only partial because users must design formulas/scripts themselves; none of it is a built-in group-generator workflow.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent export and sharing surface: copy/paste, CSV, XLSX, PDF/print, share links, collaboration, version history, and integration with the broader Google ecosystem. Output is where Sheets is much stronger than most simple web randomizers.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. There is no ad-tech utility-page mess inside Sheets, and Workspace for Education has serious privacy terms. But rosters live in Google cloud under an account, sharing permissions can leak data if misconfigured, and consumer Google account data handling is not the same as a local/browser-only tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: A Google account is required for the normal workflow, and organizational users may need Workspace permissions. Not a no-account web splitter.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic formula-based random groups are roughly normal random-splitter quality. Better results are possible with custom formulas/scripts, but there is no built-in solver, repeat minimization, constraint validation, or diagnostics.

pros: Good for saved rosters and notes
Can be extended with formulas, templates, and Apps Script
cons: No built-in constraints or repeat optimizer
Privacy depends on Google account/admin/sharing setup
tools.reviewNote: Review as Google Sheets used directly for grouping, not as a marketplace add-on or custom app. Credit platform flexibility and exports, but keep feature/result scores below purpose-built group generators because the grouping logic is user-built.

tools.name: GoOnlineTools Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Simple random groups with a modern UI, regenerate, statistics, and possible copy/download/share icons
tools.summary: GoOnlineTools Random Group Generator is a polished basic web splitter. Paste names, choose by groups or by members, generate, regenerate, and see simple statistics such as total groups, total members, average per group, and size range. The page advertises advanced options like custom team names, exclusions, representative selection, and export/share, but the visible tested workflow is still much closer to a simple random splitter than a serious constraint tool. Privacy is the main weakness because the page carries ads, Funding Choices, Google sign-in scripts, Microsoft Clarity, and other tracking/storage surface.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required for generation; sign-in exists for the broader GoOnlineTools site.
tools.overallComment: A decent modern simple splitter, but the privacy/ad-tech load is bad and the advertised advanced controls are not enough to make it a solver.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy paste-and-generate flow with by-groups/by-members modes and a clear regenerate button.
ratingComments.design: Modern and readable, though ads and surrounding tool-site chrome add clutter.
ratingComments.features: Better than the bare minimum: by groups/by members, regenerate, statistics, and advertised advanced options. Still no proven hard constraints, repeat optimization, or real diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Result action icons and page copy indicate copy/download/share, but output formats are not as clear or strong as CSV/PDF-focused tools.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The page runs ads/Funding Choices, Google account/sign-in scripts, Microsoft Clarity, and stores ad/recent/favorite/color-mode data. The policy also allows ad cookies and IP collection.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generation works without sign-in. Sign-in is visible for site features such as favorites.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above bare random because by-members mode and stats help, but assignments are still random-only unless the advanced options prove more substantial.

cons: Advanced claims feel stronger than the visible product
tools.reviewNote: Treat the marketing claims for advanced options cautiously; the verified workflow is a basic random grouping tool.

tools.name: Vertex42 Random Group Generator Template
tools.bestFor: Spreadsheet users who want a downloadable Excel/Google Sheets random grouping template
tools.summary: Vertex42 is a spreadsheet template rather than a normal web app. It gives you an Excel or Google Sheets random group generator with a names worksheet, group names, RAND-column sorting, two grouping orders, and no macros. That makes it more reusable than many throwaway web splitters, and the page is unusually honest that it does not solve the social golfer/repeat-minimization problem. It is still just a spreadsheet workaround, not a dedicated optimizer.
tools.pricing: Free template for private use. Downloadable Excel file and Google Sheets option; no account required for the template page.
tools.overallComment: A useful spreadsheet workaround with honest documentation. Better than many web toys for people already in Excel, but not a serious group-generation product.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not as fast as a web splitter because you need to download/copy a spreadsheet and understand the RAND-sort workflow. Easy enough for spreadsheet users.
ratingComments.design: The template page is dated but clear, and the spreadsheet screenshot explains the workflow well.
ratingComments.features: Useful spreadsheet basics: editable roster, group names, random sorting, two grouping layouts, and custom sorting. No constraints, attributes, repeat optimization, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong because the result lives in Excel or Google Sheets and can be copied, printed, saved, or exported through spreadsheet tools.
ratingComments.privacy: Roster data can stay in your own spreadsheet after download, which is good. The website page itself still runs ads and Google tags.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the template page. Google Sheets use may involve a Google account.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above plain web randomizers because spreadsheet sorting gives manual control, but still random-only and explicitly not social-golfer solving.

pros: Honest about not solving social golfer repeat minimization
tools.reviewNote: Scope this as a spreadsheet template, not a hosted generator.

tools.name: ClassDojo Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Teachers already using ClassDojo who want quick classroom groups
tools.summary: ClassDojo Group Maker is not really a standalone web group generator. It is a Toolkit feature inside the ClassDojo app for teachers with a ClassDojo class roster. It can randomly generate student groups of any size and supports sets of students who should not be grouped together. For existing ClassDojo classrooms that is handy; for everyone else it is an account/app workflow around a basic random classroom splitter.
tools.pricing: ClassDojo Toolkit is advertised as free and included in the ClassDojo app. ClassDojo also has optional paid/premium family features, but the Group Maker workflow itself is positioned as free.
tools.overallComment: Useful if your class already lives in ClassDojo, but weak as a general-purpose group generator. The keep-apart sets are real value; the app/account wall and limited export surface are the problem.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: For an existing ClassDojo teacher with a roster, group generation is probably very easy. For anyone else, it starts with downloading/signing into ClassDojo and setting up a class, which is a lot of friction for one random split.
ratingComments.design: ClassDojo is polished, classroom-friendly, and much nicer than most randomizer pages. The public Group Maker page is clear, but it mostly advertises the app rather than exposing the tool directly.
ratingComments.features: Better than the weakest random splitters because it uses a saved class roster and supports keep-apart sets. Still limited: random groups only, group size choices, no attribute balancing, no multi-session repeat minimization, no solver diagnostics, and no serious planning controls.
ratingComments.outputs: Very weak for export. The visible product positioning is in-app classroom display/use, not CSV, Excel, Zoom breakout export, share links, PDFs, or reusable result reports.
ratingComments.privacy: ClassDojo has a much more serious student-privacy posture than ad-heavy utility sites: COPPA/FERPA/GDPR language, iKeepSafe certifications, Student Privacy Pledge, no selling/renting, and no student behaviorally targeted advertising. But it is still a hosted classroom platform collecting rosters, account data, device/usage data, and service-provider analytics, so it is not comparable to local/no-account tools.
ratingComments.accountFriction: High. The Group Maker is part of the ClassDojo app, not a no-login webpage. You need the ClassDojo ecosystem and likely a teacher account/class roster for real use.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above a plain random splitter because keep-apart sets can prevent bad pairings. It still does not optimize balance, preferences, repeated encounters, or multi-session coverage.

pros: Keep-apart sets for students who should not be grouped together
Strong education privacy posture compared with ad-heavy utilities
cons: Mostly useful only for existing ClassDojo classrooms
tools.reviewNote: Scope this to the Toolkit Group Maker, not the full ClassDojo classroom platform. Do not let ClassDojo polish or broader classroom features inflate the group-generator score. Unknown review-builder feature states were resolved conservatively because the public Group Maker positioning does not show custom team names, clipboard export, or saved result history.

tools.name: Fun Group Names Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: A content-site group randomizer if the embedded script works in your browser
tools.summary: Fun Group Names Random Team Generator is a working content-site group randomizer with more controls than the weakest random splitters. Paste a list, process it, choose 1–20 groups, randomize groups, re-randomize members, edit the list, randomize themed group names, and use the advertised TXT/PDF download workflow. It is still basic random splitting and the page is wrapped in heavy ad/consent tracking, but the tool itself should not be treated as broken.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A useful basic splitter with fun group names and advertised downloads, dragged down hard by a noisy ad-tech wrapper and lack of real balancing.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The intended workflow is easy: paste names, process list, choose group count, randomize. Extra edit/re-randomize controls are understandable.
ratingComments.design: The widget is usable, but it sits inside a generic WordPress/content page with a lot of surrounding article/ad noise.
ratingComments.features: Above the bare minimum for a random splitter: 1–20 groups, edit list, re-randomize members, random themed group names, and download workflow. No constraints, balancing attributes, saved rosters, or repeat planning.
ratingComments.outputs: TXT/PDF downloads are advertised and the page exposes a download workflow, which is better than copy-only tools. No spreadsheet/CSV, share link, or integration-specific export.
ratingComments.privacy: Very poor. The page loads a large ad/consent stack including consentmanager-style storage, Journey/Mediavine/OpenX/Criteo/ID5/Amazon ad scripts, Clarity, Grow, and many localStorage/cookie identifiers. Not a good place for classroom rosters.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even distribution. Useful for one-off splits, but no meaningful balancing or optimization.

tools.reviewNote: Do not mark this as non-working. The generator works when the page scripts are allowed to load; judge it as a basic random splitter with extra naming/download conveniences and a very poor privacy footprint.

tools.name: Group Assigner
tools.bestFor: Quick classroom or workshop groups with random team names and focus mode
tools.summary: Group Assigner is a simple free random team generator with a nicer interface than most bare utilities. Paste names, choose Auto-Optimize or a specific group count, assign groups, shuffle, use Focus Mode, and it gives the teams random names. It stores names/results/preferences in the browser for the next visit. The weak spots are output/export and privacy: there is no serious export workflow, and the page runs Google ads/Funding Choices.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A good simple splitter with better UX than average, but still random-only and ad-supported.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy: paste names, pick auto or a group count, assign. Shuffle and focus mode are obvious.
ratingComments.design: Clean enough, with random team names and a useful focus mode. Affiliate/ad content adds noise.
ratingComments.features: Auto-optimize, group-count choices, random team names, shuffle, focus mode, and browser-saved state. No constraints, attributes, leaders, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Mostly screen presentation. Focus mode helps display, but there is no strong copy/download/CSV workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Names are said to stay in the browser and local storage confirms that. The downside is Google ads/Funding Choices and ad cookies on the page.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above the weakest splitters because auto-optimize picks sensible group counts, but assignments are still random-only.

tools.reviewNote: Random team names are a UX feature, not evidence of deeper assignment quality.

tools.name: Online Stopwatch Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Classroom presentation-style random groups with animated reveals
tools.summary: Online Stopwatch offers a collection of themed random group generators. The core workflow is basic: paste comma- or newline-separated names, choose either maximum number of groups or maximum group size, generate random groups, reveal them with animations, then view all groups. It has useful presentation/export extras like fullscreen mode, copy-to-clipboard, CSV download, jump-to-group, sound/transition toggles, and premium saved lists, but the grouping logic is still random-only.
tools.pricing: Free with ads. Premium removes ads and adds account features such as saved lists/My Page; the premium page advertises one-off access options and group accounts, with cookies required for premium login.
tools.overallComment: Good classroom showmanship around a basic random splitter. The exports and fullscreen presentation are useful, but the tool has no real grouping intelligence and the ad/tracking/privacy story is weak.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic workflow is not hard: paste names, choose a number, generate. The old animated UI, settings cog, iframed app, and many themed variants make it less clean than the best simple splitters.
ratingComments.design: Fun for classrooms, but busy and dated. The site is full of navigation, premium prompts, ads, animations, sounds, and themed variants. That is presentation value, not a clean modern group-generator UI.
ratingComments.features: Decent for a basic random splitter: number-of-groups mode, max-group-size mode, animated reveals, fullscreen, sound/transition toggles, jump-to-group, copy, CSV export, and premium saved lists. No constraints, balancing, repeats, attributes, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Better than many simple randomizers. It can copy one group or all groups, download a CSV, show all groups together, use fullscreen presentation, and save lists/results through premium workflows. No Zoom-specific export, spreadsheet import, PDF/image export, or serious report format.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The free site is ad-supported and uses Google Analytics/Google Tag Manager, Google AdSense-style advertising, Publift/Fuse ad scripts, cookies, Cloudflare, and premium account storage. The user-data terms explicitly warn not to upload personal/sensitive data and to treat uploaded data as non-sensitive/non-personal.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generating groups does not require an account. Premium is only needed for saved lists, ad-free use, and account convenience features.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even-ish sizes. Animations, themes, and stronger randomness presentation do not improve assignment quality.

pros: No account required for basic use
Premium saved lists for repeated rosters
cons: No constraints, balancing, or repeat optimization
User-data terms warn against personal/sensitive data
tools.reviewNote: Do not over-credit the many themed generators; they are mostly skins around the same random splitter. The CSV/copy/fullscreen output surface is real and should be credited.

tools.name: WordMint Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Simple classroom groups with a copy-to-clipboard button
tools.summary: WordMint Random Group Generator is a basic but functional classroom splitter. Paste names, choose number of groups or count per group, generate, and copy the results to the clipboard. That is enough for quick classroom use, and the UI is cleaner than many randomizer pages. It is still random-only and the surrounding WordMint site has account, pricing, analytics, Facebook pixel, and PostHog tracking surface.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required for the group generator; the broader WordMint product has accounts and paid plans.
tools.overallComment: A decent simple splitter with copy output. Nothing advanced, but less painful than many tools in this tier.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy: paste names, choose number of groups or count per group, generate.
ratingComments.design: Clean, readable, and focused enough, despite the broader WordMint navigation.
ratingComments.features: Basic but complete for a simple splitter: number-of-groups mode and count-per-group mode. No constraints, leaders, balancing, repeats, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy to Clipboard is useful. No CSV, spreadsheet export, print-specific view, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Weak. No account is required, but the page loads Google tags, Facebook pixel, and PostHog-related storage. The broader privacy policy is for a commercial puzzle/product site.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the generator.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping only.

pros: Cleaner UI than many randomizer pages
tools.reviewNote: Review the free generator page, not WordMint’s broader puzzle/test-making product.

tools.name: Games4esl Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Teachers who want quick random classroom groups and a printable PDF
tools.summary: Games4esl Random Group Generator is a classroom-focused random splitter. Paste names, choose a group size from 2 to 10, generate groups, and download a PDF. It also describes a leftover-handling choice for uneven splits. The actual grouping is still basic random assignment, but PDF output gives it a practical classroom advantage over many bare page-only splitters.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A good teacher-friendly basic splitter with PDF output. The ad/consent stack is the big downside.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy classroom flow: paste names, choose group size, generate. The cookie/consent overlay gets in the way.
ratingComments.design: Readable and teacher-focused, with less chaos than many old randomizer pages. Still embedded in a busy content site.
ratingComments.features: Group-size mode, 2–10 size choices, and uneven-leftover handling are useful basics. No constraints, balancing, saved lists, or repeat planning.
ratingComments.outputs: Better than most simple splitters because it has a PDF download. No CSV/spreadsheet export or share link.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The page loads Google ads/AdSense, Google Analytics, Ezoic/gatekeeper consent scripts, and Cloudflare analytics, and the policy names Google, Amazon, and Ezoic advertising partners.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random classroom grouping. Fine for one-off teams, not for fairness beyond group sizes.

tools.reviewNote: Score the teacher tool itself, not Games4esl’s broader ESL resource library.

tools.name: RakkoTools Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Basic random groups with CSV export
tools.summary: RakkoTools is a better-than-average basic random group generator. It can divide by number of teams or members per team, select organizers/representatives, and export CSV. That makes it more useful than the pure toy randomizers, but it is still random-only and the page has a bad consent/ad-tech environment.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool. No account or payment is needed for the basic generator; pricing is not the issue, the weak privacy/ad-tech environment is.

The appeal is presentation: names get placed into groups by a magic-hat animation, with fullscreen and premium/ad-free hooks. As a group generator, it is thin: enter names, choose group count, watch the animation. The surrounding Online Stopwatch page is extremely ad-heavy and tracker-heavy.
tools.pricing: Free with ads. Premium/ad-free and saved-name convenience are promoted by the site.
tools.overallComment: Fun classroom theater, weak group-generation product. The ad-tech load is severe.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The concept is simple, but the generator is embedded in a cluttered, ad-heavy page and animation adds friction if you just need results.
ratingComments.design: The magic-hat theme is fun for a classroom screen, but the surrounding page is messy and dated.
ratingComments.features: Basic animated random grouping only. Premium can save names, but there are no constraints, balancing, imports, exports, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Mostly visual/on-screen. Fullscreen helps presentation; there is no serious copy/CSV/export workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Terrible for student rosters. The page loads a large ad stack with Google ads, Amazon ads, prebid, Funding Choices, and multiple tracking/storage identifiers.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The basic generator is free, but saved names/ad-free convenience pushes toward premium.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping. The animation does not improve assignment quality.

pros: No account needed for basic use
tools.reviewNote: Treat this as an animated classroom randomizer, not as a planning tool.

tools.name: PickJa Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Quick no-account random groups with group-count or members-per-group mode
tools.summary: PickJa Random Group Generator is a clean, basic splitter on PickJa’s randomizer site. Paste one name per line, choose either number of groups or members per group, and it displays balanced random groups with member counts. The tool itself is easy and useful for one-off grouping, but there are no copy/export controls, no constraints, no saved rosters, no repeat planning, and the page is wrapped in heavy advertising/consent infrastructure.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required for the generator; sign-in exists for the broader PickJa site.
tools.overallComment: A pleasant basic splitter, but still shallow. It gains points for dual mode and readable output, then loses them on missing exports and severe ad-tech privacy posture.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Straightforward one-name-per-line paste flow with group-count and members-per-group modes. The consent banner is intrusive, but the core tool is easy once reachable.
ratingComments.design: Modern, readable, and cleaner than many randomizer pages. The surrounding navigation, ads/consent layer, and long SEO content keep it from feeling polished.
ratingComments.features: Basic splitter feature depth: paste names, choose number of groups or members per group, preview counts, and regenerate. No constraints, balancing attributes, imports, saved rosters, repeats, or diagnostics beyond counts.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy button, CSV/spreadsheet export, print/PDF/image export, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The tool page loads Google ad/analytics infrastructure and shows a consent prompt referencing 210 partners, device storage/access, personalized ads/content measurement, and precise geolocation. The policy also covers account/share data and Google Analytics. Not appropriate for sensitive rosters.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The generator works without an account. Sign-in is present for the broader PickJa site but not required for this workflow.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Normal random split quality. It balances group sizes evenly, but has no constraints, repeat minimization, seeded reproducibility, or quality diagnostics.

cons: No saved roster or result history
Heavy ad-tech consent banner and tracking environment
tools.reviewNote: Review the specific random group generator page, not PickJa’s broader randomizer/list product.

tools.name: Random Group Generator Chrome Extension
tools.bestFor: Chrome users who want a tiny local random-group popup
tools.summary: Random Group Generator is a Chrome extension, not a normal web app. The listing describes a simple popup workflow: enter one name per line, choose the number of groups, click Generate, view labeled groups, and clear the saved list. It persists the entered names in browser local storage and the Chrome Web Store disclosure says the developer does not collect or use user data. I could inspect the listing with Playwright, but did not install/run the extension itself in the browser session.
tools.pricing: Free Chrome extension. No account advertised, but it requires installing a browser extension.
tools.overallComment: A basic random splitter packaged as a Chrome extension. The local-data posture is good, but the grouping feature set is very thin and installation is unnecessary friction for most users.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The described workflow is simple, but needing to install a Chrome extension just to split groups is extra friction compared with a web page.
ratingComments.design: Only the Chrome Web Store listing and screenshots were available here. It appears to be a small functional popup, not a polished planning interface.
ratingComments.features: Very basic: one-name-per-line input, number of groups, random generation, clear, and local-storage persistence. No group-size mode, constraints, balancing, imports, exports, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Weak. The listing mentions on-screen labeled groups only. No copy, CSV, print, share, or spreadsheet export is advertised.
ratingComments.privacy: Strong on paper for a simple tool: the Chrome Web Store disclosure says the developer does not collect or use data, and names are saved locally in browser storage. Still, this is a small extension from a non-trader developer, so trust depends on installing extension code.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account advertised, but extension installation is a real barrier compared with no-install web tools.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping only. Local storage and extension packaging do not improve assignment quality.

pros: Names stored locally in browser storage
Chrome Web Store disclosure says no data collection
tools.reviewNote: Playwright inspected the Chrome Web Store listing after rejecting Google consent. Extension itself was not installed/tested; review should stay scoped to advertised/listing behavior.

tools.name: RandomLists Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Simple random teams from a pasted list
tools.summary: RandomLists is a straightforward random team splitter. It looks much cleaner than ClassTools and does the basic job quickly: paste names, choose group count, randomize, edit, rerun, and share URL state. It is still only a randomizer with very little control, and shared links put the entered names directly in the URL.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool. No paid group-generation tier is needed, but the cost is heavy ad/consent friction and a very basic feature set.
tools.overallComment: Better than ClassTools, but still just a basic random splitter with bad privacy. Its search visibility is much stronger than the product deserves.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple enough, but the Edit Settings flow plus consent/ad clutter keeps it from being a true 5/5.
ratingComments.design: Fairly clean and modern compared with ClassTools, though still a generic ad-supported utility site.
ratingComments.features: It has paste names, arbitrary group count, rerun, edit settings, and URL state. Still no real grouping intelligence.
ratingComments.outputs: Shareable URL state is useful, but it exposes entered names in the query string and there is no CSV, PDF, print, spreadsheet, or Zoom-friendly export.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad for anything sensitive. The consent banner mentions personal data sharing with 210 partners, precise geolocation, ad personalization, and device storage. The privacy policy also says information may be shared or sold for marketing. Shared links include the entered names directly in the URL.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping only. Fine for a quick one-off split, but no balancing, constraints, repeat reduction, or diagnostics.

tools.reviewNote: Like ClassTools, it undeservedly ranks extremely high for many related searches. The search visibility is much stronger than the actual tool. Generated share URLs include roster names in the query string.

tools.name: UltimateSolver Random Groups
tools.bestFor: Privacy-conscious basic random groups with cryptographic server randomness
tools.summary: UltimateSolver Random Groups is a basic random group generator. It lets users enter names, choose number of groups or people per group, optionally avoid incomplete groups, save name lists in cookies, use fullscreen/animation display, and print/copy the result. The randomization is server-side using PHP random_int(), so names are sent to the server even though the site says input/output data is not stored or shared.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account or payment required.
tools.overallComment: A decent basic splitter with unusually explicit randomness documentation and privacy claims, but still only basic random grouping and server-side processing.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Straightforward once you find the controls: enter names, choose number of groups or people per group, generate. The page has many surrounding random-generator options, so it is less clean than the best simple splitters.
ratingComments.design: Functional but dated and busy. It feels like an old calculator/randomizer site, not a focused classroom/grouping product.
ratingComments.features: Low feature depth. It supports number-of-groups, people-per-group, no-incomplete-groups options, sorting/display options, saved name lists in cookies, fullscreen, and a puzzle/expert-group mode. No balancing, constraints, multi-session planning, repeats, attributes, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Basic output only: on-page result, copy-style output, print-friendly behavior, fullscreen display, and cookie-saved name lists. No CSV/Excel export, share links, Zoom export, or result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. The page says SSL is used and input/output data is not stored or shared, and there is a clear GDPR privacy policy. But the generator posts names to a server endpoint for random_int() grouping, stores saved name lists in cookies if used, and the broader site privacy policy includes Google Analytics, AdSense, YouTube, VG WORT pixels, and affiliate tracking.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account or payment required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Standard one-off random grouping. Cryptographically secure random selection is nice, but it does not improve group assignment quality beyond random even-ish splitting.

pros: Number of groups or people per group
Claims not to store/share input or output
Can save name lists in cookies
cons: Server-side processing sends names to endpoint
tools.reviewNote: Review the random-groups page specifically. Do not over-credit cryptographic randomness; for group generation quality, it is still just a random splitter.

tools.name: iLeren Groepenmaker
tools.bestFor: Dutch-speaking teachers who need a tiny no-account classroom splitter
tools.summary: iLeren Groepenmaker is a bare Dutch classroom group maker. Paste names, choose either people per group or number of groups, enter the number, and click Maak groepen. It produces simple on-page groups. There are no ads or third-party scripts on the tool page, which is a real privacy plus, but there is also no export, copy, saved roster, balancing, constraints, or polish.
tools.pricing: Free. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A bare but honest classroom splitter. Very limited, but cleaner and more private than many ad-stuffed randomizer pages.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy if you read Dutch: paste names, choose people-per-group or number-of-groups, generate.
ratingComments.design: Extremely plain Bootstrap-style utility page. Functional but visually primitive.
ratingComments.features: Only the two basic split modes. No constraints, attributes, leaders, repeat handling, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: On-page results only. No copy, download, CSV, print, share, or saved history.
ratingComments.privacy: Strong for this category. The tool page has no obvious tracking scripts, ads, cookies, or local storage.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with basic size handling. No quality controls beyond splitting people.

pros: No obvious trackers or ads on the tool page
tools.reviewNote: Dutch-language tool; review should not inflate it based on the broader iLeren edutools directory.

tools.name: Mega Seating Plan Free Random Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Teachers who already use Seating Plan class lists
tools.summary: This review is about Seating Plan's free Random Group Maker, not the full Seating Plan product or its paid Smart Group Maker. The free tool works as a very basic classroom randomizer: paste student names, choose number of groups or students per group, and generate. It is easy, but there is almost nothing beyond the random split.
tools.pricing: Free basic random group maker. The broader Mega Seating Plan product has paid plan context, but this specific simple group-maker page is free and does not expose paid grouping controls.
tools.overallComment: A working classroom random splitter with two basic split modes, but no useful export controls and a surprisingly weak privacy posture for such a small tool.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy: paste names, choose number of groups or students per group, and click make groups. The surrounding sign-in/class-list pitch keeps it slightly below the cleanest tools.
ratingComments.design: The actual widget is clear enough, but the page around it is cluttered with navigation, sign-in prompts, and long SEO copy.
ratingComments.features: Basic but not completely empty: it supports splitting by number of groups or students per group, plus account-gated class-list use. There are no real grouping rules, constraints, repeat controls, or multi-round options in the free tool.
ratingComments.outputs: Very weak. Results appear on the page, but no obvious CSV, copy, print, PDF, download, or share workflow was available in the free tool.
ratingComments.privacy: Weak for a simple pasted-name tool. The page loads analytics/marketing scripts and sets Google Analytics/Brevo-style cookies, and Seating Plan’s privacy notice explicitly lists session recording, mouse events, form interactions, file/link clicks, and other analytics data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for pasted names. Sign-in is pushed for class lists and the smarter product, so it is not quite friction-free.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random balanced groups only. Fine for a one-off split, but no constraints, repeat reduction, attribute balancing, or diagnostics.

pros: Supports number of groups or students per group
Can use saved class lists if signed in
cons: Smarter grouping is a separate product feature
tools.reviewNote: Important: this review is about the free Random Group Maker page, not the full Seating Plan product or the Smart Group Maker.

tools.name: My2lbox Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Tiny random or level-weighted groups when privacy/ad-tech tradeoffs are acceptable
tools.summary: My2lbox Random Group Generator is a small form-based group maker. It can generate random groups or weighted groups by participant level, but the workflow is clunky: participants are entered one-by-one in separate fields, extra rows must be added manually, and results are just displayed on the page. The weighted-by-level option is a real grouping feature, but the tool has almost no export or workflow support.
tools.pricing: Free. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Interesting because it has level-weighted grouping, but the UX, output surface, and privacy/ad-tech situation are weak.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not terrible, but clunky. Instead of pasting a normal list, you enter names into separate fields and manually add more participant rows. Weighted mode adds another level-selection step.
ratingComments.design: Old Bootstrap-style page with ads and awkward layout. The cookie/ad consent layer is intrusive and parts of the surrounding page/content are in French even on the English page.
ratingComments.features: Above the weakest random splitters because it supports random or weighted grouping by participant level. But that is basically it: number of groups, participant names, optional levels. No group-size mode, constraints, leaders, imports, exports, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Very poor. Results are displayed on the page only. No copy button, CSV/text download, print view, share link, saved history, or spreadsheet-friendly output was visible in testing.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The legal page says data submitted through tools is not tracked, stored, or archived after processing, which is good. But the page shows a Google-managed consent dialog mentioning 210 partners, personalized ads/content, audience measurement, device storage/access, and possible precise geolocation, plus multiple Google AdSense/Tag scripts.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than a plain random splitter when weighted-by-level mode is used, because it can spread participant levels. Still a simple heuristic/form tool with no broader constraints or repeat optimization.

pros: Legal page says tool-submitted data is not retained
Useful for quick level-balanced classroom groups
cons: No paste-list textarea; names are entered field by field
tools.reviewNote: Playwright test confirmed random grouping from individual participant fields and page-only results. Weighted-by-level is advertised and exposed in the form, but output/export remains weak.

tools.name: Repixify Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Users who specifically want leader markers and custom team names and do not care about ad-tech
tools.summary: Repixify Random Team Generator promises more than a bare splitter: number-of-teams or participants-per-team modes, custom team names, a $ prefix to mark leaders, and a Copy All Teams button. On the actual page, the form is more brittle than it should be and can get stuck in the configuration state instead of showing results. Even if the promised controls work reliably for some users, this is still a random splitter with no hard constraints, no balancing logic beyond team sizes/leaders, and no serious export surface.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Interesting leader/team-name ideas, but the tool is clunky and the privacy footprint is ugly. Not a strong recommendation.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The visible flow is more fragile than a simple random team generator should be. The number/team-name controls add friction instead of clarity.
ratingComments.design: Modern enough visually, but surrounded by a generic tool-site layout, cookie prompt, and lots of related-tool clutter.
ratingComments.features: Leader markers, custom team names, and two split modes are real features. There are still no constraints, balancing categories, imports, history, or repeat optimization.
ratingComments.outputs: The page describes a Copy All Teams button, but no CSV, PDF, image, or spreadsheet export. Output depth is still low.
ratingComments.privacy: Very poor. The page uses a CMP/ad stack with Mediavine/Journey-style scripts, consent storage, cookies, and a policy that explicitly discusses Google ad cookies and third-party ad servers.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the generator.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random-only. Leader markers may distribute marked leaders, but there is no evidence of broader assignment quality.

tools.reviewNote: Do not credit Repixify’s general AI/text-tool catalog; only the random team generator matters here.

tools.name: Breno Design Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Small rosters where you want to click-select names and make quick visual groups
tools.summary: Breno Design Group Generator is a small visual tool on a designer portfolio site. You add members one at a time, select names with checkboxes, choose the number of groups with a slider, and generate groups. It also has Shuffle, Deselect All, and Select Random Member controls. It is nicer-looking than many bare utilities, but it is inefficient for real rosters and has no paste/import/export workflow.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A cute but shallow random grouping widget. The one-at-a-time input makes it worse than many uglier but faster splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding names one by one and selecting checkboxes is tolerable for tiny lists and bad for classrooms or workshops. Paste support is the missing basic feature.
ratingComments.design: Visually cleaner and more playful than most randomizer pages, as expected from a portfolio site.
ratingComments.features: Basic grouping only: add members, select/deselect, shuffle, select random member, choose number of groups, generate. No constraints, attributes, imports, exports, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy button, CSV, print, image/PDF export, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: No account required, but there is no clear privacy page for the tool and the site loads Google Tag Manager, Microsoft Clarity, reCAPTCHA, Webflow, and other third-party scripts.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping. The select/shuffle UI does not improve assignment quality.

cons: Names must be added one at a time
Google Tag Manager, Microsoft Clarity, and reCAPTCHA scripts
tools.reviewNote: Review the specific design-tool widget, not Breno Garret’s broader design services portfolio.

tools.name: Calculate.co.nz Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: One-off random teams when page-only output is enough
tools.summary: Calculate.co.nz Random Team Generator is a plain utility on a New Zealand calculator site. Paste names, enter the number of teams, and it outputs Team 1, Team 2, and so on. It says it can handle up to 10,000 names, but it also sorts names alphabetically inside teams after assignment, which makes the result look less like a shuffled roster. There are no export controls, no group-size mode, and no real group-planning features.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Usable for a quick split, but very basic and output-poor. It belongs near the bottom of the simple random splitter tier.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple enough: paste names, enter number of teams, generate. The surrounding calculator-site navigation is more clutter than the tool needs.
ratingComments.design: Old-fashioned calculator-page design. Functional, but not especially pleasant or focused.
ratingComments.features: Just number of teams and a textarea. No group-size mode, constraints, attributes, leaders, saved rosters, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: On-page results only. No copy button, CSV, spreadsheet export, print view, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: No account required, but the page loads Google Tag Manager/Analytics and other third-party scripts. There is no strong roster-specific privacy posture on the tool page.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random assignment, then names are sorted alphabetically inside each team. That is fine for a trivial split, but it adds no assignment-quality value.

pros: Can handle large pasted lists according to the page
tools.reviewNote: This is a small calculator-site utility, not a dedicated group assignment product.

tools.name: RandomCount Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Tiny groups where adding names one at a time is acceptable
tools.summary: RandomCount Team Generator is a basic member-per-team splitter. Add each member one at a time, set members per team, and generate. It eventually produces simple Team 1/Team 2/Team 3 results, but there is no paste-a-roster textarea, no copy/export, no group-count mode, no constraints, no saved roster, and no meaningful diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A working but weak random splitter. The one-at-a-time name entry alone makes it worse than most basic paste-and-generate tools.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding members one by one is tedious compared with pasting a class list. The rest is simple enough.
ratingComments.design: Plain Bootstrap-style utility page. Usable, but not polished.
ratingComments.features: Only add/remove members and members-per-team random splitting. No paste import, group-count mode, constraints, balancing, saved lists, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Results display on the page only. No copy, CSV, PDF, share, or print-focused output.
ratingComments.privacy: The footer privacy policy is easy to find and it discloses Google Analytics/AdSense, location-derived information, generated-data language, and legal/service-provider sharing. The tool page also set Google Analytics cookies. Lighter than many ad-tech pages, but not ideal for roster data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even team size. Fine for trivial one-off use only.

pros: Not overloaded with a huge consent stack
cons: Vague privacy policy language around location and generated data
tools.reviewNote: Treat the repeated “balanced” language as basic equal-size splitting, not real balancing. Privacy policy lives at https://randomcount.in/privacypolicy.

tools.name: Terrific Tools Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: People who want a small browser tool with individual member entry and simple group-size controls
tools.summary: Terrific Tools Random Group Generator is not a serious group-planning tool. It has a group title, members-per-group setting, dummy-name option, member rows, balance/remainder toggles, and an advertised copy-to-clipboard output. The problem is the workflow is clunkier than most simple splitters because it uses individual member fields instead of a normal paste-a-list textarea, and the page is wrapped in promotional banners, comments, account buttons, and a huge directory of unrelated random tools.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required for generation; account prompts are for comments, ratings, bookmarks, and the broader site.
tools.overallComment: A weak random group generator with a few basic controls but worse input ergonomics than many simpler tools. The surrounding site noise and ad/privacy posture drag it down further.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The tool is usable, but individual member rows are a bad fit for a class or workshop roster. A simple textarea would be faster.
ratingComments.design: The actual form is readable, but the repeated launch banner, account/comment widgets, and enormous unrelated-tool grid make the page feel bloated.
ratingComments.features: Slightly above the weakest splitters: group title, members-per-group, dummy names, balance/remainder toggles, and dynamic members. Still no constraints, attributes, multi-round planning, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: The page advertises copying groups to the clipboard, but there is no spreadsheet, CSV, image, PDF, Zoom, or share workflow visible in the basic form.
ratingComments.privacy: The site says tools process data in the browser, which is good for names. But the privacy policy also discloses Google Analytics and Mediavine interest-based advertising, and the page sets CMP/ad-consent storage.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generation itself is free and no-login. The account UI is mainly for ratings, comments, and bookmarks.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random grouping only. Balance/remainder toggles help group sizes, not assignment quality.

cons: Clunky member entry for real rosters
Google Analytics and Mediavine ad/privacy footprint
tools.reviewNote: Review the specific random group generator, not the broader terrific.tools directory or desktop converter product.

tools.name: MadeInText Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Very simple one-off random groups when privacy does not matter
tools.summary: MadeInText Random Group Generator is a tiny free web tool: paste names or items, enter the number of groups, and generate. It does produce random groups, but that is the whole story. There is no group-size mode, no copy/export button for results, no constraints, no balancing, no saved roster, and no diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A working bare splitter dragged down by a heavy ad/consent stack and almost no features.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste names, enter number of groups, generate. It is extremely simple.
ratingComments.design: Plain content-site layout with lots of surrounding navigation and tool links. Functional but not pleasant.
ratingComments.features: Only number-of-groups random splitting. No group-size mode, constraints, balancing, saved lists, imports, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Results are just printed on the page. The Copy Link button shares the page, not the generated groups.
ratingComments.privacy: Terrible for classroom/student names. The page uses Mediavine/rapid-edge style ad scripts, consent storage, Google Analytics, and a policy describing AdSense, Mediavine, IP address, device, browser, email hash, and interest-based advertising data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random assignment. Fine for trivial splits, nothing more.

tools.reviewNote: Treat it as a basic text-tool-site widget, not a serious classroom group planner.

tools.name: PureTables Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Very basic page-only random teams
tools.summary: PureTables Random Group Generator is as basic as it gets: enter names one per line, enter how many teams to generate, click Generate, and read the page result. It works, but there are no output buttons, no group-size mode, no constraints, no balancing, no saved roster, and no polish. The site also uses Google AdSense/Funding Choices and a privacy policy that describes Google Analytics and ad cookies.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A working but bare random splitter. It has almost no reason to rank above cleaner or more capable alternatives.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple: paste names, enter team count, generate. It would be hard for a tool this thin to be confusing.
ratingComments.design: Plain, dated utility-page design with ads and generic site navigation.
ratingComments.features: Only names and number of teams. No group-size mode, constraints, leaders, balancing, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy, CSV, print, PDF, share, or export controls.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad. The page loads AdSense/Funding Choices and the privacy policy describes Google Analytics, IP/location/browser data, and Google ad cookies.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping only.

tools.reviewNote: This is a basic random-generator page, not a classroom planning tool.

tools.name: Sheffield Hallam TEL-e-vision Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Very quick no-account random classroom groups
tools.summary: Sheffield Hallam TEL-e-vision Group Maker is a tiny old-school HTML/JavaScript random group generator. Paste one name per line, optionally type a list of roles, choose 1–20 groups, and it shuffles names into groups in the browser. It is refreshingly direct and account-free, but it has almost no product surface beyond basic random splitting.
tools.pricing: Free web page. No account or payment required.
tools.overallComment: A bare-bones random splitter. Useful in a pinch, but it is not competitive with modern group generators and has no real planning/export capability.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very simple: paste names, choose number of groups, click the button. The only friction is the dated page and confusing optional roles field.
ratingComments.design: Very dated. Large old-style controls, table layout, document.write result page, and no modern polish. It is readable, but barely a product UI.
ratingComments.features: Almost nothing beyond random splitting by number of groups. The roles field is just displayed alongside each group rather than acting like a real assignment or constraint system. No balancing, constraints, imports, repeat handling, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Extremely weak. Results are just written to a new page view. You can manually select/print through the browser, but there is no export, copy button, CSV, spreadsheet, image, PDF, share link, or saved history.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than server-side randomizers because the grouping logic runs in page JavaScript and there is no account. However the page includes Sheffield Hallam Google Tag Manager, and the broader university privacy policy is general website/institutional tracking rather than a dedicated student-roster promise for this tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping. It makes even-ish groups by shuffling and distributing names round-robin, but there is no balancing, preferences, keep-apart logic, or repeat reduction.

cons: Roles field is not real role assignment
No group size control beyond number of groups
Google Tag Manager on the page
tools.reviewNote: The optional roles field should not be treated as role assignment; it is displayed for every group and does not change grouping logic.

tools.name: Web0 Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Ultra-minimal local random groups
tools.summary: Web0 Random Team Generator is an extremely small browser-side random group page. Paste names, choose the number of groups, click Genera Gruppi, and it displays group cards. The implementation uses client-side JavaScript and no obvious ad/analytics scripts on the tool page, but the UI is partially Italian even on the English URL and there are no output/export controls.
tools.pricing: Free. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A minimal local random splitter. Privacy/page simplicity is good, but the tool is too bare to be competitive.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy enough once you understand the Italian labels: paste names, set number of groups, generate. The language mismatch hurts clarity.
ratingComments.design: Simple Bootstrap-style layout. Not ugly, but generic and partly untranslated.
ratingComments.features: Almost no feature depth: paste names and choose number of groups. No group-size mode, constraints, balancing, leaders, exports, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy, download, CSV, print, share, or saved history.
ratingComments.privacy: Good on observed behavior. The tool page loads local JS plus jQuery/Bootstrap and the grouping code runs client-side with Math.random; no obvious ads/analytics scripts were present in the tested page HTML.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random round-robin grouping. Fine for one-off random teams, nothing more.

pros: No obvious ad/analytics scripts on tested page
cons: Partly Italian UI on English page
tools.reviewNote: Playwright test generated 3 groups from 9 names. JS inspection confirmed client-side Math.random shuffle with no fetch/localStorage/export code.

tools.name: ClassTools Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Very quick classroom group splits
tools.summary: ClassTools is one of the weakest serious-looking options in this comparison, which makes its high search visibility hard to justify. It technically works for a quick random classroom split: paste names, choose the number of groups, generate, then save/link or print. But the UI is dated, the page is cluttered, the feature set is shallow, and the privacy/ad-tech footprint is bad.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool. No account or payment is needed for the random group generator. Premium removes ads and adds broader ClassTools workspace/history benefits, but the reviewed free group-generator workflow is surrounded by monetized site clutter.
tools.overallComment: Generally, one of the weakest group generators on this list. It works, but it is a dated, ad-heavy, shallow random splitter that ranks far better than its quality deserves.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The core workflow is easy because there is almost nothing to configure: paste names, choose group count, generate. Ads, consent prompts, premium nudges, and old UI clutter keep it from being a clean 5/5.
ratingComments.design: Terrible, dated, and cluttered. The tool is usable, but the surrounding page feels like an old ad-heavy template site rather than a modern group generator.
ratingComments.features: Very shallow. It has paste names, arbitrary group count, save/link, and print, but no balancing, constraints, repeat avoidance, imports, roster management, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Save/link and print are genuinely useful and better than display-only tools. There is still no proper CSV, spreadsheet, PDF, image, or Zoom-friendly export workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad. The page shows a large consent/ad stack, including language about personal data being stored/accessed/shared with 1147 partners, plus Ezoic/Gatekeeper/Google/Funding Choices and multiple tracking identifiers. I would not enter sensitive classroom rosters here.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is required, but a premium login exists. For the basic group generator workflow, there is basically no signup friction.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random only. This is what is advertised, but nothing more: no balancing, no constraints, no repeat reduction, and no quality diagnostics.

cons: One of the weakest serious-looking options
Ranks far better than it deserves
tools.reviewNote: This tool ranks extremely high for many related searches despite being a weak, dated, ad-heavy random splitter. Keep the review blunt: its search visibility is not evidence of product quality.

tools.name: Schule Dietmannsried Gruppen Generator
tools.bestFor: Bare-bones German classroom group splitting
tools.summary: Schule Dietmannsried Gruppen Generator is a tiny German-language school utility. Paste names, choose either maximum group size or number of groups, generate, and it displays groups plus counts. It appears to be a minimal client-side page with no ads or external scripts on the tool page, but it has no copy/export workflow and almost no features beyond splitting names.
tools.pricing: Free. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A very bare-bones random splitter. Privacy/page simplicity is good, but product capability is almost nonexistent.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple if you understand German: paste names, choose mode, enter a number, click generate. The labels are minimal and not localized to English.
ratingComments.design: Extremely plain school utility page. Functional, but visually primitive.
ratingComments.features: Only two basic split modes: maximum group size or number of groups. No constraints, balancing, leaders, exports, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy button, download, CSV, print view, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Good on the tool page itself: no obvious ad/analytics scripts and the grouping appears client-side. The broader school privacy policy mentions cookies/server logs, but this specific page is lightweight.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even-ish groups. No assignment quality beyond random splitting.

pros: Maximum group size or number-of-groups mode
No obvious ad/tracking scripts on tool page
tools.reviewNote: Playwright test generated 3 groups from 9 names. The first attempted shell fill used literal backslash-n; retest with real newlines confirmed normal operation.

tools.name: Wheel of Names
tools.bestFor: Random picking, raffles, and manual group-building workarounds
tools.summary: Wheel of Names is primarily a random name picker, not a group generator. Teachers and facilitators can use it to pick names and manually assemble teams, but there is no real group-generation workflow beyond workarounds.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported spinner. No paywall for premium wheel features, but that mostly benefits random picking/presentation rather than actual group generation.
tools.overallComment: Excellent random picker, but barely a group generator. It only belongs here because people use it as a manual workaround for classroom grouping.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: For random picking, it is about as easy as it gets: paste names and spin. For actual group generation, the workflow becomes manual, but the base tool itself is very easy.
ratingComments.design: Polished, familiar, and good for live presentation. The wheel, sounds, themes, fullscreen/streaming support, and customization are much stronger than most plain utility sites.
ratingComments.features: For group generation specifically, feature depth is very low. Multi-wheel, weighted entries, themes, embedding, API access, and audio customization are useful spinner features, but they do not create balanced groups. There is no real team splitter, no group capacities, no grouping constraints, no multi-session planning, and no repeat optimization.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong output/sharing options for a wheel: short links, embeddable iframes, streaming support, local/cloud saved wheels, and API access. Weak as a group-generator output surface because it does not produce proper team rosters, CSV group assignments, Zoom exports, or schedule-style results.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. Better than the worst ad-tech utilities because it has local-file saving, automatic device-local state, explicit privacy/security documentation, and an option to close ads for the session. Still not great for sensitive classroom names: the site uses ads and Google Analytics, cloud-saved/shared wheels can put entries in Firestore/Google infrastructure, account login is needed for cloud access, and shared URL examples can include entries directly in the URL.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is needed for basic spinning, local use, or local file saving. Cloud-saved wheels and cross-device access require login, so it is not completely account-free.
ratingComments.resultQuality: As a random picker, the randomness is strong. As a group generator, result quality is barely applicable: teams have to be built manually, so there is no balancing, no constraint handling, and no repeat minimization.

pros: Google Sheets import and weighted entries
cons: Manual workaround needed for team splits
No constraints, capacities, or repeat optimization
Shared/cloud wheels can expose entered names
tools.reviewNote: Review strictly as a group-generator candidate. Wheel of Names is a strong presentation/random-picking tool, but its group-generation capability is basically a workaround: pick people one at a time and manually form teams.

tools.name: AhaSlides Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: AhaSlides users who already want random-team generation inside an interactive presentation workflow
tools.summary: AhaSlides presents this as a random team generator feature, but the public page is mostly a marketing page for the broader presentation platform. It says to try the demo or sign up to unlock more features, but the standalone generator is not clearly exposed as a normal paste-names-and-generate tool on the page. As a group-generator entry, this should be scoped narrowly: AhaSlides may be a capable presentation product, but this page is not a strong standalone group maker.
tools.pricing: AhaSlides has a free plan with limits and paid presentation plans. The pricing page describes up to 5 quiz and 3 poll slides on Free, paid upgrades for unlimited interactive slides, and special education/nonprofit rates.
tools.overallComment: Useful only if you are already in AhaSlides. As a standalone group generator, it is weak and hidden behind platform/account context.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The page does not behave like a straightforward public group generator. It pushes users toward signup and the broader platform.
ratingComments.design: The marketing page is polished, but that does not make the group generator itself good.
ratingComments.features: For group generation specifically, very little is visible. Do not give credit for unrelated presentation, quiz, poll, or AI slide features.
ratingComments.outputs: No useful standalone group output/export workflow is exposed on the public page.
ratingComments.privacy: AhaSlides has a serious privacy/security policy and GDPR/PDPA language, but the marketing page loads GTM, Microsoft Clarity/Bing, CookieScript, Zoho chat, HubSpot/Webflow-style scripts, and platform data is hosted/processed by AhaSlides.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The page explicitly pushes signup to unlock more features. Bad for a quick public group generator.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Assume basic random assignment only from the public description. No visible constraints, balancing, or optimization.

pros: Part of a real presentation platform
Security/privacy documentation is more serious than content-farm tools
cons: Standalone generator is not clearly accessible
Many marketing/analytics/chat scripts on the page
Presentation features are mostly irrelevant to group generation
tools.reviewNote: Review the random-team-generator page/feature only, not AhaSlides as an audience-engagement suite.

tools.name: Teach Starter Random Name Picker
tools.bestFor: Picking one student at a time, not generating groups
tools.summary: Teach Starter has a free random name picker/spin-wheel classroom widget, but I could not find a real random group generator from Teach Starter. It can support manual grouping only by picking names one at a time or using saved word lists; it does not split a roster into teams.
tools.pricing: Free random name picker widget; broader Teach Starter resources and saved-account workflows may sit behind free/paid teacher plans, depending on region/resource.
tools.overallComment: Not a real group generator. Keep only if the article wants to mention classroom random-picking workarounds; otherwise it is a removal candidate.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: For picking a single random name, it is easy. For generating groups, the workflow is bad because there is no group-generation workflow at all; you would have to pick names manually.
ratingComments.design: Polished and classroom-friendly compared with many utility sites. The issue is not presentation quality; it is that the relevant tool is a name picker, not a group maker.
ratingComments.features: Almost no group-generation feature depth. It can pick random names and use saved word lists, but it does not choose team count, group size, capacities, balancing, constraints, exports, or repeat avoidance.
ratingComments.outputs: No useful group output. At best it displays one selected name or spinner result. No team roster, copy/export surface, CSV, print groups, or shareable generated assignment.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than ad-tech-heavy utilities: no obvious AdSense mess on the widget page and Teach Starter has detailed education/privacy language including COPPA/PRIVO posture. Still a hosted education platform with accounts, cookies, server logs, location/IP/device data, marketing uses, analytics/customer tooling, and saved word-list/account behavior.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The random name picker is presented as free, but the broader Teach Starter ecosystem pushes account/resource workflows, and saved class lists depend on Teach Starter account features. No account is needed for the basic idea, but it is not as frictionless as a tiny standalone splitter.
ratingComments.resultQuality: No group result quality to credit. Random single-name selection may be fair, but it does not create balanced teams or group assignments.

pros: Free widget for simple random selection
Saved word lists can help teachers reuse class lists
cons: No team count or group size controls
No group roster output or export
Hosted platform collects normal account/log/cookie data
tools.reviewNote: No working Teach Starter group generator was found. The closest relevant tool is the free Random Name Picker / Spin Wheel, which is only a manual grouping workaround.
